title: World IP Day 2026: Uneven Performance or Team Europe?
content:
Download KR21’s infographic

A key justification for intellectual property rules is their contribution to innovation. The prevailing logic suggests that the creation of (time-limited) monopolies allows creators to recoup the costs of developing new ideas.

This argument is relatively straightforward for measures such as patents, trademarks, or design rights (although concerns about abuses persist). 

However, it is more complicated regarding copyright. This doesn’t just regulate the creative industries, but equally affects citizens, scientists and researchers. The research process is explicitly about accessing and building on existing facts and insights. As such, anything that causes barriers to, or exclusion from, this needs to be strictly evaluated. Copyright needs to be designed with this objective in mind. 

Picking up (tangentially) on the theme of this year’s World Intellectual Property Day – IP and sport – we’re lo

title: Every Time a Library Cannot Do Its Job, the Public Interest Loses: Tell Us Your Story
content:
The mission of libraries is under threat. Not from budget cuts or censorship alone, but from a quieter crisis: a legal and market framework that is systematically limiting libraries’ ability to function. Publishers can refuse to license digital materials. Contracts can override rights granted by law. Digital locks can make legal activities impossible. Copyright rules written for classrooms leave libraries out. The result is the same every time: a user who needed something, and a library that could not provide it.

Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) named these barriers in its action plan, Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries, launched in February 2026. Today, 23 April, on UNESCO’s World Book and Copyright Day, we are taking the next step.

Launching the #LetLibrariesBeLibraries Case Study Repository

Evidence matters in policy. Legislators and regulators need to understand not just that these problems exist in theory, but that they are happening in real libraries, to real users, right now. That is w

title: Moldova Symposium Report: Advocating to Let Libraries Be Libraries
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 25 March 2026, Mariana Harjevschi, KR21 national coordinator for Moldova, presented the KR21 roadmap “Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries” at the 35th National Symposium “The Library Year 2025”, held in Chișinău. See the recording of Mariana’s intervention.

The symposium was organised by the National Library of the Republic of Moldova under the auspices of the Ministry of Culture. The theme of this year’s edition, “The Involvement of Libraries in Digital Transformation: Opportunities and Challenges”, reflects the strategic direction of 2025 and highlights the essential role of libraries in supporting the digital transforma

title: Different Players, Same Game: What Libraries Have in Common With Gamers
content:
The European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) on the right to resell digital games is on the agenda of a joint hearing by the European Parliament’s Internal Market and Consumer Protection (IMCO), Legal Affairs (JURI), and Petitions (PETI) Committee on 16 April.

The ECI addresses a practice that many consumers know well: buying a game on the understanding that they own it, only to find it switched off or deleted by the company that sold it. Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) supports the push for action, and we have a direct interest in its outcome, because the problem the ECI identifies is not specific to games.

Libraries Face the Same Structural Imbalance

Libraries and the researchers, educators, learners, and citizens who depend on them operate under conditions that are structurally identical to those the ECI describes. Licensed digital collections disappear without warning. Access is conditional, temporary, and governed by contract terms that no individual

title: Copyright Maximalism Will Stifle a Research-Intensive Digital Economy
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published on 26 March 2026 on the Times Higher Education

On 18 March, after more than two years of deliberating what to do about artificial intelligence and copyright, the UK government has announced the need to “gather more evidence on how copyright laws are impacting the development and deployment of AI across the economy and the economic benefits of reform”.

Perhaps the reason for this delay has been minsters’ growing realisation that the issues concerning AI and copyright are far more complicated than the “Big Music v Big Tech” paradigm being presented in the media. As this

title: The ERA Act, Open Science and the Freedom to Stay
content:
As the launch of the European Commission’s proposals for the European Research Area (ERA) Act get closer, there is growing interest in how it will look to deliver on its promise to make a reality of the “Fifth Freedom”: the free movement of knowledge.

This idea was most recently championed by former Italian Prime Minister Enrico Letta in his report Much More than a Market. Letta identifies the movement of knowledge as a primary driver for EU competitiveness and hence one of the six key areas of action the EU should focus on.

Currently, discussions around this freedom focus heavily on researcher mobility, such as the recent proposals for model contracts designed to help

title: The Open Risk Facing UK Universities: A Lack of AI Sovereignty
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published in a shortened version on 11 March 2026 by Research Professional News

Universities make a uniquely important contribution to the UK’s research intensive economy, both through generating new ideas and insights through basic research, and then through their application, for example in the form of research–industry collaborations, spinouts and more.

In order to maximise productivity, like almost every other sector, the research sector is looking at how to make the most of AI technologies. Closely connected to this, UK researchers are also working to develop new models and applications of AI that can support numerous sectors of the economy including the NHS, the environment, agriculture etc.

In this context, as we see with the ongoing furore around Palantir, it is a cause for concern that we are so dependent on US technology

title: Cyprus Workshop Report: The Secondary Publication Right
content:
Blog post by Giorgos Glossiotis

On Monday, 16 March 2026, KR21 organised a workshop, with the support of the Cyprus Association of Librarians – Information Scientists (CALIS, ΚΕΒΕΠ), titled “The Secondary Publication Right: Concept, Legal Framework, and the Bulgarian Experience”, which was held in Nicosia, at the University of Cyprus Library. The event was led by Ana Lazarova, KR21 national coordinator for Bulgaria.

Following a warm welcome from CALIS President Theodora Petrou, participants were introduced to KR21’s new campaign, “Let Libraries be Libraries,” by Giorgos Glossiotis, KR21 national coordinator for Greece and Cyprus.

Deep Dive: Secondary Publishing Rights & the Bulgarian Experience

title: 14 April Webinar | Closed Licences, Closed Doors: Open Licensing, AI, and the Future of Research Impact
content:
Watch the 14 April 2026 webinar recording

When?

  • Date: 14 April 2026
  • Time: 11:00 – 12:30 CEST / 10:00 – 11:30 BST

What?

Open licensing and copyright are important aspects of publishing research, but what do they mean in practice? Creative Commons (CC) licences offer a standardised framework for sharing research outputs, with open access being increasingly mandated by international funders. However, growing concern about research outputs being used to train generative AI tools has led some authors to seek more restrictive licences. Yet the question remains: Does a more restrictive licence stop AI from using your work, or does it simply limit the collaborations driving innovation?

Europe PMC, partners of EMBL-EBI, and KR21 invite you to a joint webinar tackling this pressing question.
